message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T91 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Branch: implement/T91    Base commit: 55ff983 (current main HEAD)
headline: Cap provisional unbound-source demux state; lazy peerState instantiation and dead-peer teardown
description: Bound the bootstrap DoS surface and pin the per-peer lifecycle (Q26): cap the provisional/unbound-source tracking state (small, drop-on-exhaustion) separately from the steady-state peerState map, which is sized to the static configured peer set. Instantiate the HEAVY per-peer state (the ~2048-frame resequencer ring, FEC encoder/decoder, scheduler, per-(peer,path) probers) LAZILY on first authenticated source->peer binding rather than at Open; tear it down when that peer's WG session/liveness is gone (wired from device peer events), freeing the ring and FEC buffers; a torn-down configured peer re-instantiates cleanly on its next authenticated PROBE. On cap exhaustion, drop new unbound-source state; NOTHING ever evicts a LIVE peer. (Build on the T83-T89 multi-peer demux: peerState struct, peerBySource atomic CoW+CAS, demuxInbound trial-decode binding.)
acceptance: Tests (fake clock where needed): a flood of many distinct spoofed unbound source addresses cannot grow demux state past the configured cap and cannot evict or disturb a live bound peer; peerState heavy fields are absent before first authenticated binding, instantiated on binding, torn down after session/liveness loss, and re-instantiate + pass traffic on re-bind; a live (Up) peer is never torn down regardless of other peers' churn. `go test ./internal/bind/...` passes.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test -race ./internal/bind/... && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes.

If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t91-fix implement/T91` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit there, report the EXACT sha. Mutation-verify any new test discriminates. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

125	//
126	// It EMBEDS its *sharedPathState so the socket identity (name/id/src/conn) is reached
127	// transparently, and back-references the owning peerState so a receive handler resolves
128	// exactly that peer's resequencer/reflector/FEC decoder.
129	type peerPathState struct {
130		*sharedPathState
131		peer  *peerState
132		codec *frame.Codec
133		// prober is this (peer,path)'s own probe initiator (nil when the bind runs without the
134		// probe transport). It is set at path creation and immutable for the path's life,
135		// so the Bind-owned receive goroutine reaches it via the peerPathState it already
136		// holds — NOT through a shared, dynamically-mutated probers slice — which is
137		// what keeps echo handling race-free while paths are added/removed at runtime (T30).
138		prober *telemetry.Prober
139	
140		// txBytes/rxBytes are cumulative OUTER-wire byte counters for this (peer,path), the
141		// per-path traffic accounting the /metrics exposition reports (T23). txBytes counts
142		// the DATA-frame wire bytes this path egresses on the Send hot path; rxBytes counts
143		// every outer datagram this path's readLoop receives (DATA, PROBE, and echo alike —
144		// the true received wire volume). They are atomics so the send/receive hot paths
145		// increment them WITHOUT taking m.mu (lock-free), and the scrape/snapshot path reads
146		// them with a plain atomic Load; a single writer per counter (Send holds m.mu while
147		// choosing the path, and each path has exactly one readLoop) means the Add is
148		// uncontended in the common case. "Bytes on Starlink vs 5G" — the data-thrift signal
149		// (requirement 2) — is exactly txBytes: when the weighted scheduler collapses to the
150		// primary under sub-capacity load, the backup path's Send count stays ~flat.
151		txBytes atomic.Uint64
152		rxBytes atomic.Uint64
153	
154		mu        sync.Mutex
155		remote    netip.AddrPort
156		hasRemote bool
157	}
158	
159	// peerState holds the per-PEER datapath state that was a process-global singleton before
160	// the multi-peer concentrator split: the SINGLE virtual endpoint the engine holds for this
161	// peer, the peer's send scheduler, its own outer-seq space, its shared send Codec, its
162	// probe reflector, its receive resequencer and FEC send/receive planes, its per-path probe
163	// initiators, and its per-(peer,path) views over the shared sockets. The single-peer
164	// edge/hub constructs EXACTLY ONE peerState (so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split
165	// singleton — Multipath embeds it as the primary and the datapath reaches its fields through
166	// that embed); the concentrator constructs one peerState per bound peer. resequencer and
167	// fecRecv stay atomic.Pointer, and outerSeq atomic, so the lock-free receive/send fast paths
168	// read them WITHOUT m.mu.
169	type peerState struct {
170		// name is the peer id/name, the key under which Multipath.peersByName holds this peer.
171		// Empty on the single-peer edge/hub (there is only one peer to key).
172		name string
173	
174		// psk is THIS peer's effective pre-shared key: the sole seam from which every frame
175		// Codec this peer derives (its sendCodec and each per-(peer,path) receive codec, via
176		// newCodec) and its probe Reflector authenticate. The single-peer edge/hub sets it to
177		// the one configured psk (so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton);
178		// the concentrator sets a DIFFERENT psk per bound peer, so one peer's codec/reflector
179		// rejects another peer's frames (T84). NewCodec/NewReflector PSK-derivation itself is
180		// unchanged — only WHICH psk each peer feeds them differs.
181		psk config.Key
182	
183		// Immutable per-peer collaborators, built at construction and persisting across the
184		// Open→Close socket lifecycle (the concentrator pins virt's destination once for the
185		// process life, so virt in particular must NOT be recreated per Open).
186		virt      *udpEndpoint
187		scheduler sched.Scheduler
188		reflector *telemetry.Reflector
189		// newProber mints a prober for a path admitted to THIS peer at runtime (T30); nil
190		// disables runtime path addition for the peer (a bind without the probe transport).
191		newProber ProberFactory
192		// probers is this peer's BOOT-TIME per-path probe initiator set, in durable-membership
193		// (m.defs) order — bound AND deferred — shared with this peer's scheduler. At Open each
194		// bound entry is bound onto its peerPathState (pp.prober), and thereafter the hot paths
195		// reach a path's prober through the peerPathState — never by indexing this slice — so a
196		// runtime path add/remove cannot race echo handling. Nil when the peer runs without the
197		// probe transport; a nil here also gates the whole probe loop off.
198		probers []*telemetry.Prober
199	
200		// Per-Open state, (re)built by Open and cleared by Close.
201		sendCodec *frame.Codec
202		paths     []*peerPathState
203		outerSeq  atomic.Uint64
204		// resequencer is this peer's shared T18 receive resequencing buffer. Published
205		// atomically so the per-path readLoop goroutines read it WITHOUT m.mu.
206		resequencer atomic.Pointer[reseq.Resequencer]
207		// FEC datapath (T24), per peer. fecSend accessed only under m.mu; fecRecv published
208		// atomically like resequencer. Both nil/empty when FEC is off.
209		fecSend *fecSender
210		fecRecv atomic.Pointer[fecReceiver]
211	}
212	
213	// newPeerState builds the durable per-peer datapath state whose probe Reflector — and,
214	// through ps.psk, every frame Codec this peer later derives in Open/AddPath (its sendCodec
215	// and each per-(peer,path) receive codec, via newCodec) — authenticate under THIS peer's
216	// psk. It is the seam that replaces the pre-split single-psk assumption (T84): the
217	// single-peer edge/hub mints exactly one (the primary) from the sole configured psk, while
218	// the concentrator mints one per bound peer from that peer's DIFFERENT effective psk, so a
219	// peerState built from a different psk gets its own codec/reflector and cross-psk frames are
220	// rejected. virt is created here (not per Open) because the concentrator pins its destination
221	// once for the process life. The Reflector draws its per-path challenges from crypto/rand,
222	// exactly as before. The per-Open fields (sendCodec, paths, resequencer, FEC planes) are
223	// left zero for Open to (re)build.
224	func newPeerState(name string, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler, newProber ProberFactory, probers []*telemetry.Prober) *peerState {
225		return &peerState{
226			name:      name,
227			psk:       psk,
228			virt:      &udpEndpoint{},
229			scheduler: scheduler,
230			reflector: telemetry.NewReflector(psk, rand.Reader),
231			newProber: newProber,
232			probers:   probers,
233		}
234	}
235	
236	// newCodec derives a fresh frame Codec bound to THIS peer's psk — the send Codec at Open and
237	// each per-(peer,path) receive Codec share the derivation but not the instance, since a Codec
238	// is not safe for concurrent use (each receive path decodes on its own goroutine). Deriving
239	// from ps.psk (not a Multipath-wide key) is what makes a path's receive Codec the codec of the
240	// peer the path is bound to (T84).
241	func (ps *peerState) newCodec() (*frame.Codec, error) {
242		return frame.NewCodec(ps.psk)
243	}
244	
245	// deferredPath is a configured path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr was not yet
246	// assignable at Open (net.ListenUDP -> EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the
247	// address, e.g. a 5G modem with no DHCP lease at boot). Rather than tear the whole
248	// bond down, Open records the path here instead of binding it: the tunnel comes up
249	// on the paths that DID bind, and this path stays DOWN — its prober, never fed an
250	// echo, reports StateDown, which the scheduler excludes from Pick, exactly as the
251	// runtime path-down model treats a live-but-silent path. It carries the boot-time
252	// prober so the T55 background reconcile that retries the bind reuses the SAME
253	// path-id stamp instead of minting a new one. prober is nil only on a bind without
254	// the probe transport, which never defers (see Open).
255	type deferredPath struct {
256		def    config.Path
257		prober *telemetry.Prober
258	}
259	
260	func (ps *peerPathState) setRemote(ap netip.AddrPort) {
261		ps.mu.Lock()
262		ps.remote, ps.hasRemote = ap, true
263		ps.mu.Unlock()
264	}
265	
266	func (ps *peerPathState) getRemote() (netip.AddrPort, bool) {
267		ps.mu.Lock()
268		defer ps.mu.Unlock()
269		return ps.remote, ps.hasRemote
270	}

272	// Multipath is the P1 bonding conn.Bind: one UDP socket per configured path
273	// (bound to the path's source address), all fronted by a SINGLE stable virtual
274	// endpoint the engine holds per peer. Send wraps each opaque WireGuard datagram
275	// in an outer DATA frame (own outer-seq + path-id) and picks a path; one
276	// Bind-owned reader per path unwraps DATA frames into the shared resequencer, and
277	// a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc drains it and hands the inner WG datagram up
278	// under the shared virtual endpoint (the fan-in that lets paths be added/removed at
279	// runtime without the engine spawning receive goroutines — T30). It replaces
280	// bind.Passthrough behind the same conn.Bind seam (device wiring is unchanged).
281	//
282	// Lifecycle: the sockets live for the duration of an Open→Close span, NOT the
283	// Multipath's whole life. The amneziawg engine calls Close() before every Open()
284	// (device.upLocked → BindUpdate → closeBindLocked, and on IpcSet listen_port /
285	// route-change events) and cycles Close↔Open on Down/Up, so — exactly like
286	// conn.StdNetBind — Open creates the per-path sockets and Close tears them down
287	// AND clears the path state so the next Open rebuilds from scratch. The "closed"
288	// state is simply "no bound sockets" (len(paths)==0); there is no separate sticky
289	// flag that a later Open would have to reset.
290	//
291	// Path policy: an injected sched.Scheduler (T15) chooses the egress path per
292	// datagram. The P1 MVP wires an active-backup scheduler (all traffic on the
293	// preferred primary, transparent failover to a backup on a T13 path-DOWN signal
294	// with failback hysteresis); the weighted/FEC-aware policy (T21) is a different
295	// Scheduler swapped in here with no Bind change. No FEC/resequencing yet
296	// (P2/P3), but every DATA frame carries its outer-seq and path-id so T18/T24 can
297	// consume them.
298	// ProberFactory mints a *telemetry.Prober for a path admitted at runtime (T30),
299	// stamped with the given stable path-id. The device wires it capturing the SAME
300	// per-boot session id, prober config, clock, PSK, and logger the boot-time probers
301	// were built with, so a runtime path's liveness is measured identically to a
302	// boot-time path (its probes join the same session so the peer's reflector adopts
303	// them without a challenge reset). It is nil on a bind built without the probe
304	// transport (the T12 unit tests), which therefore cannot add paths at runtime.
305	type ProberFactory func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober
306	
307	type Multipath struct {
308		defs []config.Path
309	
310		// classify maps each outbound datagram to its pacer traffic class from the inner
311		// WireGuard message type, parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia obfuscation profile
312		// so a WireGuard control frame (handshake/keepalive) is recognised — and pacing-
313		// exempted — under advanced security too, not only in vanilla mode (defect D22). It
314		// is immutable after construction and holds no lock, so Send reads it off m.mu.
315		classify wgClassifier
316	
317		// deferredListen binds a reconciled deferred path's socket (T55 background
318		// reconcile). It is an injection seam: the default pins the source IP (net.ListenUDP,
319		// matching AddPath's runtime bind), and a test overrides it to drive the
320		// deferred→bound transition deterministically — a source_addr "becoming assignable"
321		// — without a real interface address having to appear on the host. Immutable after
322		// construction, never nil.
323		deferredListen func(src netip.Addr, port uint16) (*net.UDPConn, error)
324	
325		mu sync.Mutex
326	
327		// The PRIMARY peer, embedded so the single-peer datapath (Send, the receive drainer,
328		// the probe loop) and the existing single-peer tests reach its fields — virt,
329		// scheduler, reflector, newProber, probers, sendCodec, paths, outerSeq, resequencer,
330		// fecSend/fecRecv — transparently through promotion, keeping behaviour byte-identical
331		// to the pre-split singleton. It is peers[0]; the concentrator's additional peers live
332		// in peers/peersByName. The former process-global resequencer/outerSeq/scheduler now
333		// live on this peerState, NOT on Multipath.
334		*peerState
335	
336		// peers is every bound peer (len 1 on the single-peer edge/hub). The runtime shared-
337		// path add/remove fan-out iterates it so per-(peer,path) state is created/torn-down for
338		// EVERY currently-bound peer (attachSharedPathLocked / RemovePath). peersByName keys
339		// them by peer id/name.
340		peers       []*peerState
341		peersByName map[string]*peerState
342		// peersView is the LOCK-FREE snapshot of the bound peer set the single engine-facing
343		// receive drainer iterates (newReceiveFunc). m.peers is mutated only under m.mu (peer
344		// wiring / fan-out); every mutation republishes this pointer (republishPeersLocked) so
345		// the drainer enumerates EVERY bound peer's resequencer WITHOUT taking m.mu on the
346		// receive hot path — the same lock-free-publish discipline resequencer/fecRecv use. It
347		// is never nil after construction (the constructor publishes the primary-only view).
348		peersView atomic.Pointer[[]*peerState]
349		// peerByEndpoint is the inbound endpoint-keyed demux placeholder (still unused today: the
350		// single-peer edge/hub needs no demux, and the source-keyed binding below is what the
351		// concentrator receive path routes on).
352		peerByEndpoint map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState
353		// peerBySource is the inbound source-demux map: a learned source address → the peer it was
354		// bound to by an authenticated PROBE (T88). The concentrator's per-socket readLoops resolve
355		// a datagram's source to its owning peer through it; a source is bound ONLY on the first
356		// PROBE that MAC-verifies under a peer's psk (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned
357		// only from authenticated PROBEs). It is published copy-on-write through an atomic.Pointer
358		// so the receive hot path resolves a bound source with a lock-free Load (no m.mu — a reader
359		// must never block on m.mu, since Close waits on the readers WHILE holding it), and a new
360		// binding is installed lock-free by a CAS republish of a copy with the entry added
361		// (bindSourceToPeer). Nil until the first binding; the single-peer edge/hub never consults
362		// it (one peer owns every socket — handleInbound's fast path skips the demux entirely).
363		peerBySource atomic.Pointer[map[netip.Addr]*peerState]
364		// peerByVirt routes an OUTBOUND Send to its owning peer: the engine hands Send the
365		// single virtual endpoint (*udpEndpoint) it holds for a peer, and this map resolves
366		// that pointer to the peer's datapath state (outerSeq, scheduler, sendCodec, fecSend,
367		// per-(peer,path) set). It is the SEND-side dual of peerByEndpoint/peerBySource (which
368		// demux INBOUND datagrams). Each peer's virt is DISTINCT, so the lookup is exact; an
369		// endpoint not in this map is an unknown peer, which Send refuses rather than
370		// misrouting onto some other peer's paths. The primary is registered at construction;
371		// the concentrator registers each additional peer as it binds it. Read under m.mu on
372		// the Send path; written under m.mu (or at single-threaded construction).
373		peerByVirt map[*udpEndpoint]*peerState
374	
375		// shared is the SHARED per-socket path list (the sockets themselves), rebuilt from
376		// m.defs on every Open and mutated by the runtime add/remove. Each bound peer holds a
377		// peerPathState VIEW over a subset of these; on the single-peer edge/hub primary.paths
378		// is index-aligned with shared. len(shared)==0 is the "closed" (no sockets) state.
379		shared []*sharedPathState
380	
381		// deferred holds the configured paths whose well-formed source_addr was not yet
382		// assignable at the last Open (EADDRNOTAVAIL). They are NOT in shared or the
383		// scheduler — the tunnel runs on the paths that bound — but are recorded here,
384		// index-independent of shared, for the T55 background reconcile to retry as their
385		// addresses appear. Rebuilt from scratch on every Open; guarded by m.mu. The deferred-
386		// path machinery is SHARED (per-socket): a promoted deferred path fans its per-(peer,
387		// path) state out to every peer exactly as a runtime AddPath does.
388		deferred []deferredPath
389		// defaultRemote is the fallback per-path remote (the peer's wireguard
390		// endpoint) applied to any path without its own dest_addr. It may be set by
391		// ParseEndpoint BEFORE Open, so it is stored here and applied at Open time.
392		defaultRemote    netip.AddrPort
393		hasDefaultRemote bool
394	
395		// fecCfg / adaptiveCfg are the FEC configuration (T24/T29), shared by every peer; the
396		// per-peer fecSend/fecRecv runtime state Open builds from them lives on peerState. nil
397		// when FEC (respectively adaptive FEC) is disabled — the datapath is then byte-for-byte
398		// the pre-T24 behaviour. See fec.go.
399		fecCfg      *fec.Config
400		adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config
401	
402		// Receive fan-in (T30). To let a path be added at runtime WITHOUT the engine
403		// spawning a new receive goroutine (it only builds its receive goroutines once,
404		// from the ReceiveFuncs Open returns), the per-path socket reads are decoupled
405		// from the engine-facing delivery: the Bind owns one readLoop goroutine PER path
406		// (tracked by readersWG) that reads its socket and feeds the shared resequencer,
407		// and Open returns a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc that drains the resequencer
408		// in order. A reader pokes deliverSignal after each datagram so the drainer wakes;
409		// recvClosed is closed by Close to release both the drainer and any reader parked
410		// on a delivery. openPort mirrors Open's bind port so a runtime-added path binds
411		// consistently; nextPathID is a monotonic high-water that persists ACROSS Open
412		// spans (a Close->Open never lowers it; only a process restart resets it), so a
413		// surviving path is never renumbered and a freed id is never reused for the
414		// process lifetime, and thus can never collide with the peer's per-path reflector
415		// state.
416		deliverSignal chan struct{}
417		recvClosed    chan struct{}
418		readersWG     sync.WaitGroup
419		openPort      uint16
420		nextPathID    uint16
421	
422		// Receive-path liveness sweep (T39, defect D15). Liveness DOWN-detection normally
423		// rides StartProbeLoop's single wall-clock ticker goroutine (emitProbes → Tick).
424		// Under heavy CPU load — e.g. the concentrator absorbing a saturating forward flood
425		// on 4 vCPU — that timer goroutine can be scheduled with ~1s jitter, delaying a
426		// path-DOWN transition (and thus the reply-direction failover) past the P1 budget.
427		// The per-path receive goroutines, in contrast, are the goroutines the inbound
428		// traffic is ALREADY scheduling, so driving Tick from them re-evaluates liveness
429		// even when the timer goroutine is starved. sweepIntervalNanos is the probe
430		// interval (0 until StartProbeLoop arms it — a bind without the probe transport
431		// never sweeps); lastSweepNanos is the wall-clock high-water throttling the sweep
432		// to at most once per interval so the receive hot path stays cheap.
433		sweepIntervalNanos atomic.Int64
434		lastSweepNanos     atomic.Int64
435	}
436	
437	// compile-time proof that Multipath satisfies the engine's Bind contract.
438	var _ Bind = (*Multipath)(nil)
439	
440	// NewMultipath returns a closed multipath Bind over the configured paths; call
441	// Open to bind the per-path sockets. The PSK keys the outer framing and must be
442	// set (config validation guarantees it). The scheduler is the injected send-side
443	// path-selection policy (T15) whose priority order MUST match the paths slice
444	// order (index 0 = the preferred primary); it is a required collaborator so the
445	// send path is never without a policy.
446	//
447	// probers is the per-path probe initiator that drives on-wire liveness (T13/T37).
448	// When non-nil it MUST hold exactly one *telemetry.Prober per path, in path order,
449	// and those SAME values MUST be the scheduler's PathHealth sources so the liveness
450	// the probe loop measures is the liveness the scheduler selects on. Pass nil to
451	// run the bind without the probe transport (the T12 unit tests drive selection via
452	// sched.AlwaysUp instead). A Reflector is built from the PSK to answer peer probes.
453	//
454	// newProber is the factory the runtime path-add path (AddPath, T30) uses to mint a
455	// prober for a newly-admitted path; it must be non-nil to allow AddPath and MUST be
456	// paired with a non-nil probers (the boot-time set) — a factory without a boot-time
457	// slice would let AddPath append to a nil m.probers, breaking the m.paths/m.probers
458	// alignment invariant and panicking on the next Open at m.probers[i]. Pass nil to
459	// forbid runtime path addition.
460	//
461	// fecCfg is the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon FEC configuration (T24). Pass nil to run the
462	// datapath with FEC disabled (the pre-T24 behaviour, byte-for-byte); a non-nil,
463	// pre-validated config turns the send-side parity encoder and receive-side recovery
464	// decoder on. It is validated here (fail fast) so an invalid ratio is rejected at
465	// construction rather than at the first Open.
466	// adaptiveCfg is the adaptive-FEC controller configuration (T29). Pass nil for the
467	// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24); a non-nil config REQUIRES a non-nil fecCfg (the
468	// controller resizes the FEC encoder's parity — it is meaningless with the plane off)
469	// and reinterprets fecCfg.ParityShards as the controller's parity ceiling. It is
470	// validated here (fail fast) so a mis-tuned control law is rejected at construction.
471	//
472	// amnezia is the tunnel's AmneziaWG obfuscation profile (config.Amnezia). It
473	// parameterizes the send-path frame-type classifier so a WireGuard control frame is
474	// recognised and pacing-exempted under advanced security — custom magic headers and
475	// handshake junk prefixes — as well as in vanilla mode (defect D22). Pass the zero value
476	// for a vanilla (unobfuscated) tunnel; the classifier then uses the default type words
477	// and no junk prefix. It does NOT need to match the config's validated state — an
478	// all-zero profile is exactly the vanilla classifier.
479	func NewMultipath(paths []config.Path, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler, probers []*telemetry.Prober, newProber ProberFactory, fecCfg *fec.Config, adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config, amnezia config.Amnezia) (*Multipath, error) {
480		if len(paths) == 0 {
481			return nil, errors.New("bind: at least one path is required")
482		}
483		if !psk.IsSet() {
484			return nil, errors.New("bind: PSK is required for outer framing")
485		}
486		if len(paths) > 256 {
487			// path-id is a uint8 in the DATA frame header.
488			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: at most 256 paths supported, got %d", len(paths))
489		}
490		if scheduler == nil {
491			return nil, errors.New("bind: a send scheduler is required")
492		}
493		if newProber != nil && probers == nil {
494			// A runtime-path factory without a boot-time prober slice would let AddPath append
495			// to a nil m.probers, desyncing m.paths from m.probers and panicking on the next
496			// Open at m.probers[i]. The two are paired by construction; enforce it here.
497			return nil, errors.New("bind: newProber requires a non-nil probers (boot-time set)")
498		}
499		if probers != nil && len(probers) != len(paths) {
500			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: probers must have one entry per path (got %d, want %d)", len(probers), len(paths))
501		}
502		for i, pr := range probers {
503			if pr == nil {
504				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: prober %d is nil", i)
505			}
506		}
507		if fecCfg != nil {
508			if err := fecCfg.Validate(); err != nil {
509				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: invalid FEC configuration: %w", err)
510			}
511			if fecCfg.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
512				// A deadline this large makes every deadline-flushed group's recovery
513				// structurally late: the resequencer skips the gap (resequencerTimeout) before
514				// the parity-derived frames land, so recovery never delivers. Reject it rather
515				// than ship a bond whose FEC silently cannot help (defect #4).
516				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: fec deadline %s exceeds the max %s (must stay safely below the resequencer's %s per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped)", fecCfg.Deadline, maxFECDeadline, resequencerTimeout)
517			}
518		}
519		if adaptiveCfg != nil {
520			if fecCfg == nil {
521				return nil, errors.New("bind: adaptive FEC requires a FEC configuration (the controller resizes the FEC encoder's parity)")
522			}
523			if err := adaptiveCfg.Validate(); err != nil {
524				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: invalid adaptive FEC configuration: %w", err)
525			}
526			// The controller's parity ceiling (MaxParity) is the fixed FEC ParityShards both
527			// ends agree on: the receiver's decoder is built at fecCfg.ParityShards, and the
528			// encoder must never emit more parity than that ceiling (SetParity clamps to it).
529			// A mismatch would let the controller target a parity index the decoder rejects,
530			// so bind the two together at construction rather than trusting the caller.
531			if adaptiveCfg.MaxParity != fecCfg.ParityShards {
532				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: adaptive FEC parity ceiling (MaxParity=%d) must equal the FEC parity_shards (%d), which the receiver's decoder is built at", adaptiveCfg.MaxParity, fecCfg.ParityShards)
533			}
534			if adaptiveCfg.DataShards != fecCfg.DataShards {
535				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: adaptive FEC DataShards (%d) must equal the FEC data_shards (%d)", adaptiveCfg.DataShards, fecCfg.DataShards)
536			}
537		}
538		// The single-peer edge/hub constructs EXACTLY ONE peerState (the primary). virt is
539		// created here — NOT per Open — because the concentrator pins its destination once for
540		// the process life and every existing test relies on the virtual-endpoint pointer being
541		// stable across Open/Close/add/remove.
542		primary := newPeerState("", psk, scheduler, newProber, probers)
543		m := &Multipath{
544			defs:           append([]config.Path(nil), paths...),

544			defs:           append([]config.Path(nil), paths...),
545			classify:       newWGClassifier(amnezia),
546			deferredListen: defaultDeferredListen,
547			peerState:      primary,
548			peers:          []*peerState{primary},
549			peersByName:    map[string]*peerState{primary.name: primary},
550			peerByEndpoint: map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState{},
551			peerByVirt:     map[*udpEndpoint]*peerState{primary.virt: primary},
552			fecCfg:         fecCfg,
553			adaptiveCfg:    adaptiveCfg,
554		}
555		// Publish the initial (primary-only) peer view the receive drainer iterates. No
556		// concurrency yet — the bind is not open — so this runs without m.mu.
557		m.republishPeersLocked()
558		return m, nil
559	}
560	
561	// republishPeersLocked snapshots m.peers into the lock-free peersView the engine-facing
562	// receive drainer (newReceiveFunc) iterates. The caller MUST hold m.mu whenever the bind
563	// can be open (so the snapshot never races a concurrent m.peers append); the constructor
564	// is the sole exception (it runs before the Multipath is reachable by any goroutine). The
565	// snapshot is a fresh slice so a later append to m.peers never mutates a view the drainer
566	// is mid-iteration over.
567	func (m *Multipath) republishPeersLocked() {
568		snap := make([]*peerState, len(m.peers))
569		copy(snap, m.peers)
570		m.peersView.Store(&snap)
571	}
572	
573	// Open binds one UDP socket per configured path to that path's source address on
574	// port (0 = random per socket), sets a large SO_RCVBUF on each, spawns one
575	// Bind-owned reader per path, and returns a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc (the
576	// fan-in drainer) plus the first path's bound port. Each path whose config carries
577	// a dest_addr — or, failing that, the peer's wireguard endpoint learned via
578	// ParseEndpoint — starts with a known remote; the rest are learned from inbound
579	// traffic.
580	//
581	// Open is the sole creator of the per-path sockets: the engine's bring-up path
582	// calls Close() first (on the still-unopened bind) and then Open(), so building
583	// the sockets HERE — not in NewMultipath — is what makes that Close→Open sequence
584	// (and every subsequent Down→Up) work. The engine passes the previously-bound
585	// port back on a re-Open; each path binds to it on its own distinct source
586	// address, and the first path's bound port is returned so the engine keeps a
587	// stable listen port across the cycle (matching conn.StdNetBind).
588	func (m *Multipath) Open(port uint16) ([]ReceiveFunc, uint16, error) {
589		m.mu.Lock()
590		defer m.mu.Unlock()
591		if len(m.paths) != 0 {
592			return nil, 0, conn.ErrBindAlreadyOpen
593		}
594	
595		// (Re)build the per-Open datapath planes — send Codec, receive resequencer, and FEC
596		// send/receive state — fresh for this bring-up, for EVERY bound peer (not just the
597		// primary via promotion). This keeps Open symmetric with closeSocketsLocked, which
598		// clears every peer's per-Open state: a concentrator peer bound before Open must get
599		// its OWN fresh planes on each Close→Open cycle, and one peer's (re)creation must never
600		// touch another peer's release point or FEC group state. On the single-peer edge/hub
601		// m.peers holds only the primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split rebuild.
602		// See openPeerDatapathLocked.
603		for _, p := range m.peers {
604			if err := m.openPeerDatapathLocked(p); err != nil {
605				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
606				return nil, 0, err
607			}
608		}
609	
610		// Resolve every path's interface up front and decide, per path, whether its
611		// socket may be device-bound (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard — survives a T16
612		// re-roam) or must pin the specific source IP (the pre-T16 behaviour, required
613		// when paths share an interface or the interface is multi-address, so distinct
614		// specific-IP sockets coexist on one port without an EADDRINUSE collision). See
615		// selectDeviceBinds.
616		srcs := make([]netip.Addr, len(m.defs))
617		for i := range m.defs {
618			srcs[i] = m.defs[i].SourceAddr
619		}
620		bindDevs := planPathBinds(srcs)
621	
622		// A path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr is merely NOT-YET-ASSIGNABLE at boot
623		// (EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the address — a 5G modem without a DHCP
624		// lease, Starlink mid-obstruction) is DEFERRED rather than treated as fatal, so
625		// the bond comes up on the paths that DO bind (G2/W1, approach A). Tolerating it
626		// requires the runtime path-down machinery: the deferred path's prober stays
627		// StateDown so the scheduler excludes it, and a DynamicScheduler whose membership
628		// can be reconciled to only the bound paths. Absent the probe transport (the T12
629		// no-prober unit binds) or a dynamic scheduler there is no such Down model, so —
630		// exactly as AddPath refuses a runtime add without probers — every bind error
631		// stays fatal there. A MALFORMED source_addr never reaches here (config.validate
632		// rejects it at load), and any OTHER bind error (EADDRINUSE, permission) is fatal.
633		dyn, dynOK := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler)
634		tolerateDefer := m.probers != nil && dynOK
635		m.deferred = nil
636	
637		actualPort := port
638		firstBound := true
639		// boundProbers is index-aligned with m.paths (the paths that actually bound), so
640		// the scheduler reconcile below and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping stay aligned
641		// even when a middle path deferred. nil in the no-prober case (nothing to reconcile).
642		var boundProbers []*telemetry.Prober
643		for i := range m.defs {
644			def := m.defs[i]
645			// Device-bind this path when selectDeviceBinds proved it safe (so a
646			// mid-session source-address change / T16 re-roam does not break the socket),
647			// otherwise pin the specific source IP. See selectDeviceBinds / listenPath.
648			c, err := listenPath(def.SourceAddr, port, bindDevs[i])
649			if err != nil {
650				if tolerateDefer && errors.Is(err, syscall.EADDRNOTAVAIL) {
651					// Defer this path: record its def + boot prober (kept Down) for the T55
652					// background reconcile to retry, and leave the bond to come up on the rest.
653					// Mirrors AddPath's rollback discipline — a failed path never disturbs the
654					// tunnel — at the boot boundary.
655					m.deferred = append(m.deferred, deferredPath{def: def, prober: m.probers[i]})
656					continue
657				}
658				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
659				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: open path %q on %s: %w", def.Name, def.SourceAddr, err)
660			}
661			// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort: the kernel caps at net.core.rmem_max and
662			// SetReadBuffer does not require privilege, so a returned error is rare;
663			// treat it as non-fatal rather than refusing to bind in a restricted env.
664			_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
665	
666			// The path binds to the primary peer here; its receive codec is that peer's codec
667			// (reached through the embedded primary, as the datapath reaches m.sendCodec).
668			codec, err := m.newCodec()
669			if err != nil {
670				_ = c.Close()
671				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
672				return nil, 0, err
673			}
674	
675			shared := &sharedPathState{name: def.Name, id: uint8(i), src: def.SourceAddr, conn: c}
676			ps := &peerPathState{sharedPathState: shared, peer: m.peerState, codec: codec}
677			if m.probers != nil {
678				ps.prober = m.probers[i]
679				// Reconcile the DATA-frame path-id to the prober's IMMUTABLE stamp rather than
680				// to the slice index i: after a runtime RemovePath the survivor keeps its
681				// original (higher) stamp, so index-based numbering would renumber a live path
682				// AND diverge its DATA id from its PROBE stamp. Taking id from the prober keeps
683				// DATA and PROBE agreeing on the wire and a survivor's id stable across a reopen.
684				shared.id = ps.prober.PathID()
685				boundProbers = append(boundProbers, ps.prober)
686			}
687			switch {
688			case def.DestAddr.IsValid():
689				ps.setRemote(def.DestAddr)
690			case m.hasDefaultRemote:
691				ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
692			}
693			m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
694			m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
695			// Publish the primary's view for the receive demux (T88). On the single-peer edge/hub
696			// this is the ONLY view, which handleInbound reads as the byte-identical fast path; a
697			// concentrator peer bound later appends its own view to the same socket.
698			shared.addViewLocked(ps)
699			if firstBound {
700				actualPort = uint16(c.LocalAddr().(*net.UDPAddr).Port)
701				firstBound = false
702			}
703		}
704	
705		// Hard guard (a): a bond with NO transport is impossible. If every configured path
706		// deferred, tolerance must NOT degrade to a zero-path bind — fail fatally, exactly
707		// as the pre-tolerance Open did when the sole path could not bind.
708		if len(m.paths) == 0 {
709			_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
710			return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: no configured path could bind its source address (all %d deferred as not-yet-assignable)", len(m.deferred))
711		}
712	
713		// nextPathID is a MONOTONIC HIGH-WATER carried ACROSS Open spans, never decreased.
714		// Close does not reset it, and here it is only ever RAISED to at least max(existing
715		// path stamp)+1. Resetting it to len(m.paths) (the pre-fix behaviour) re-minted an id
716		// a survivor still held once a RemovePath had opened a gap in the stamp space below
717		// that survivor's stamp: the next runtime AddPath then collided with the live path at
718		// an identical (PathID, SessionID), and because the peer's Reflector keys anti-replay
719		// AND the session challenge PER PathID, the strict-monotonic replay filter dropped one
720		// of the two independent ProbeSeq streams -> probe loss / false-DOWN. Deriving the
721		// high-water from the (prober-stamped) ps.id covers both the probe-transport case and
722		// the T12 no-prober case (where ps.id == i, so the high-water is len(m.paths)).
723		m.openPort = port
724		for _, ps := range m.paths {
725			if uint16(ps.id)+1 > m.nextPathID {
726				m.nextPathID = uint16(ps.id) + 1
727			}
728		}
729		// A DEFERRED path's boot prober already OWNS its path-id stamp; raise the
730		// high-water past it too, so a runtime AddPath arriving while the path is still
731		// deferred cannot mint that reserved id and collide with it once T55 binds it.
732		for _, dp := range m.deferred {
733			if dp.prober != nil {
734				if uint16(dp.prober.PathID())+1 > m.nextPathID {
735					m.nextPathID = uint16(dp.prober.PathID()) + 1
736				}
737			}
738		}
739	
740		// Reconcile the scheduler's membership with the path slice just rebuilt from
741		// m.defs. A runtime AddPath/RemovePath (T30) keeps m.defs, m.probers, AND the
742		// live scheduler in lockstep during an Open span; re-pinning the scheduler's
743		// health list to the BOUND probers HERE — index-aligned with m.paths, since each
744		// bound path appended its ps.prober to boundProbers in order above — is the single
745		// reconciliation point that makes that runtime membership survive this
746		// Close→Open cycle without a scheduler/path desync (no frozen zombie health
747		// entry, no resurrected removed path). A DEFERRED path (not-yet-assignable
748		// source_addr) is deliberately EXCLUDED here: its prober is absent from the
749		// scheduler, so Pick can never select it, and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping
750		// stays index-aligned with the bound-only path slice. Only meaningful with the
751		// probe transport: a bind without probers cannot change membership at runtime
752		// (AddPath is refused) and never defers, so its scheduler is left exactly as built.
753		if m.probers != nil && dynOK {
754			health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(boundProbers))
755			for i, pr := range boundProbers {
756				health[i] = pr
757			}
758			if err := dyn.SetPaths(health); err != nil {
759				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
760				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: reconcile scheduler on open: %w", err)
761			}
762		}
763	
764		// Stand up the receive fan-in: one Bind-owned reader per path feeding the shared
765		// resequencer, and a single engine-facing drainer. Both channels are recreated
766		// per Open so a Close→Open cycle starts clean.
767		m.deliverSignal = make(chan struct{}, 1)
768		m.recvClosed = make(chan struct{})
769		for _, ps := range m.paths {
770			m.readersWG.Add(1)
771			go m.readLoop(ps, m.deliverSignal)
772		}
773		// Deadline-tick goroutine for FEC group close (T24): it flushes a partial group's
774		// parity on time under low load so a group is never stranded waiting for the size
775		// threshold. Tracked by readersWG (like the readers) so Close waits for it; it
776		// exits on recvClosed. It TryLocks m.mu, so it never blocks Close's readersWG.Wait
777		// held under m.mu.
778		if m.fecSend != nil {
779			m.readersWG.Add(1)
780			go m.fecTickLoop(m.fecCfg.Deadline, m.recvClosed)
781		}
782		return []ReceiveFunc{m.newReceiveFunc(m.deliverSignal, m.recvClosed)}, actualPort, nil
783	}
784	
785	// openPeerDatapathLocked (re)builds ONE peer's per-Open datapath planes: its send Codec
786	// (derived from THIS peer's psk), its receive resequencer, and — when FEC is configured —
787	// its FEC send/receive planes. Each is fresh per Open so a Close→Open cycle (or reconnect)
788	// re-pins the peer's release point and re-anchors its FEC grouping to this bring-up rather
789	// than a stale prior span. It writes ONLY the given peer's fields (from the peer's own psk
790	// and the bind-wide fecCfg/adaptiveCfg), so one peer's (re)creation never touches another
791	// peer's resequencer or FEC group state — the per-peer lifecycle boundary that keeps a
792	// reconnect on one peer from disturbing another's. Caller holds m.mu; on error the caller
793	// unwinds the whole Open via closeSocketsLocked (which clears every peer's per-Open state),
794	// so a partial build here is cleaned up.
795	func (m *Multipath) openPeerDatapathLocked(ps *peerState) error {
796		sendCodec, err := ps.newCodec()
797		if err != nil {
798			return err
799		}
800	
801		// A fresh resequencer per Open: its release point re-pins to the first frame THIS peer
802		// receives after this bring-up, so a Close→Open cycle (or reconnect) never wedges on a
803		// stale high-water outer-seq. Published atomically so the peer's per-path readLoop

1075	// readLoop reaches it through demuxInbound, which first resolves the owning view on a shared
1076	// concentrator socket (on the single-peer edge/hub the reader's own view is the owner, so
1077	// demuxInbound's fast path calls this directly — byte-identical to the pre-concentrator
1078	// behaviour). Delivery up the WG
1079	// path is deferred to the resequencer (Pop, in the engine-facing drainer): a DATA
1080	// frame is not handed up here but pushed into the shared resequencer to be released in
1081	// outer-seq order.
1082	//
1083	//   - DATA: the decoded inner datagram is pushed into the resequencer keyed by
1084	//     the frame's outer-seq (delivered later, in order, via Pop). The path's
1085	//     remote is NOT learned here — remote-learning is authenticated-only (see
1086	//     below), so a forged DATA frame cannot steer a path's return endpoint.
1087	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=false: an authenticated peer probe. Its source is learned as
1088	//     the path's remote (D11: a probe-only backup path gets a usable return remote
1089	//     before any DATA flows) and it is reflected straight back to that source via
1090	//     this path's socket (T13 Reflector). Reflection writes independently of the
1091	//     scheduler/getRemote so an echo returns even on a not-yet-active path.
1092	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=true: an authenticated echo of one of our own probes. Its
1093	//     source is learned as the remote too, and the raw echo is fed into this
1094	//     path's Prober (HandleEcho) to update RTT/loss and drive liveness.
1095	//   - anything else (PARITY/CONTROL/malformed): dropped.
1096	//
1097	// Remote-learning and reflection touch only authenticated (MAC-verified) PROBE
1098	// frames — Decode has already verified the tag for the PROBE kind — which is what
1099	// resolves D9: an attacker who forges an unauthenticated DATA frame can no longer
1100	// repoint a path's return endpoint.
1101	//
1102	// FEC seam (T24): DATA ingestion via resequencer.Observe is keyed purely on
1103	// outer-seq, so an FEC decoder can slot in BEFORE the resequencer with no change
1104	// here — PARITY (dropped today) will feed the decoder, which reconstructs missing
1105	// DATA frames and calls Observe with their ORIGINAL outer-seq, identical to a
1106	// natively-received frame.
1107	func (m *Multipath) handleInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1108		fr, err := ps.codec.Decode(raw)
1109		if err != nil {
1110			return // drop malformed / PSK-mismatched outer frames
1111		}
1112		m.dispatchInbound(ps, fr, raw, srcAP)
1113	}
1114	
1115	// demuxInbound is the readLoop's per-frame entry on a (possibly shared) socket: it resolves
1116	// which bound peer's view owns the datagram, then hands the frame to handleInbound on THAT
1117	// view (T88). Because the readLoop holds only the PRIMARY's view of a shared socket (one
1118	// reader per socket), the routing that a concentrator needs — a datagram from peer B decoded
1119	// and resequenced under peer B's plane — happens HERE, not by trusting the reader's own view.
1120	//
1121	// Fast path: a socket with a single peer view (the edge/hub, or any socket not shared by a
1122	// second peer) needs no demux — dispatch on the reader's own view, byte-identical to the
1123	// pre-concentrator behaviour. The per-socket view snapshot is read lock-free; a nil/one-entry
1124	// snapshot means "not a shared concentrator socket".
1125	//
1126	// Shared socket (>1 view):
1127	//   - A source already bound by a prior authenticated PROBE routes straight to that peer's
1128	//     view of THIS socket (one lock-free map Load). A frame that does not verify under that
1129	//     peer's psk — a spoofed source it cannot forge the MAC for — is dropped by handleInbound.
1130	//   - An unbound source is trial-decoded against each peer's psk-derived codec (O(peers),
1131	//     bounded by the static peer count). Only an authenticated PROBE establishes a binding
1132	//     (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned only from authenticated PROBEs), and a
1133	//     PROBE's MAC verifies under EXACTLY ONE psk — so the FIRST psk whose codec yields a PROBE
1134	//     identifies the peer, binds the source, dispatches, and the loop STOPS there. A non-PROBE
1135	//     decode does NOT stop the trial: DATA/PARITY carry no MAC and are forgeable by design, so
1136	//     a genuine PROBE from a later peer can cross-psk-garble into a DATA/PARITY kind under an
1137	//     earlier peer's codec (~0.4%); the loop must therefore `continue` past a non-PROBE decode
1138	//     to give every remaining psk its chance to authenticate a PROBE. A non-PROBE decode
1139	//     carries no binding authority and is dropped either way — a genuine DATA/PARITY from a
1140	//     not-yet-bound source never dispatches or binds until that peer's PROBE binds it, so
1141	//     continuing past it changes nothing for genuine frames. A forged/garbage frame verifies
1142	//     as a PROBE under NO psk, binds nothing, and is dropped cheaply.
1143	func (m *Multipath) demuxInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1144		views := ps.views.Load()
1145		if views == nil || len(*views) <= 1 {
1146			m.handleInbound(ps, raw, srcAP)
1147			return
1148		}
1149		if bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(srcAP.Addr()); ok {
1150			for _, v := range *views {
1151				if v.peer == bound {
1152					m.handleInbound(v, raw, srcAP)
1153					return
1154				}
1155			}
1156			return // the bound peer holds no view of this socket (removed): drop
1157		}
1158		for _, v := range *views {
1159			fr, err := v.codec.Decode(raw)
1160			if err != nil {
1161				continue // not this peer's psk — try the next
1162			}
1163			if _, isProbe := fr.(frame.Probe); !isProbe {
1164				// Decoded under this psk but not a PROBE (DATA/PARITY carry no MAC, so
1165				// this may be a genuine PROBE from a later peer that cross-garbled into an
1166				// unauthenticated kind here). No binding authority: try the remaining psks
1167				// rather than aborting — a genuine unbound DATA/PARITY still never binds.
1168				continue
1169			}
1170			m.bindSourceToPeer(srcAP.Addr(), v.peer)
1171			m.dispatchInbound(v, fr, raw, srcAP) // already decoded: dispatch without re-decoding
1172			return
1173		}
1174		// No peer's psk verified: a forged/garbage frame. No binding, drop.
1175	}
1176	
1177	// lookupPeerBySource resolves a learned source address to the peer bound to it, or nil when
1178	// the source is not yet bound. It reads the copy-on-write binding map with a single lock-free
1179	// Load, so the concentrator receive hot path never takes m.mu (see peerBySource).
1180	func (m *Multipath) lookupPeerBySource(addr netip.Addr) (*peerState, bool) {
1181		mp := m.peerBySource.Load()
1182		if mp == nil {
1183			return nil, false
1184		}
1185		p, ok := (*mp)[addr]
1186		return p, ok
1187	}
1188	
1189	// bindSourceToPeer records addr→p in the source-demux map, installed lock-free by a CAS
1190	// republish of a copy with the entry added (T88). It takes NO lock: a reader must never block
1191	// on m.mu (Close waits on the readers WHILE holding m.mu), so the binding — written from a
1192	// readLoop goroutine — must not acquire it. Idempotent: an already-present addr→p binding is a
1193	// no-op, and a lost CAS (a concurrent bind on another socket) simply retries. Copy-on-write
1194	// keeps every published map immutable, so a concurrent lookupPeerBySource over the old snapshot
1195	// is never disturbed.
1196	func (m *Multipath) bindSourceToPeer(addr netip.Addr, p *peerState) {
1197		for {
1198			old := m.peerBySource.Load()
1199			if old != nil {
1200				if existing, ok := (*old)[addr]; ok && existing == p {
1201					return
1202				}
1203			}
1204			var n int
1205			if old != nil {
1206				n = len(*old)
1207			}
1208			next := make(map[netip.Addr]*peerState, n+1)
1209			if old != nil {
1210				for k, v := range *old {
1211					next[k] = v
1212				}
1213			}
1214			next[addr] = p
1215			if m.peerBySource.CompareAndSwap(old, &next) {
1216				return
1217			}
1218			// Lost the race with a concurrent bind: reload and retry.
1219		}
1220	}
1221	
1222	// dispatchInbound handles one already-decoded inbound frame on the resolved peer's view (ps):
1223	// it routes to that peer's resequencer / FEC decoder / reflector. The source demux in
1224	// demuxInbound has already selected ps so a shared socket serving many peers resequences each
1225	// peer's stream against that peer's own buffer; on the single-peer edge/hub ps.peer is the
1226	// embedded primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton. raw is retained for
1227	// the probe transport (HandleEcho / Reflect re-decode it under the peer's psk).
1228	func (m *Multipath) dispatchInbound(ps *peerPathState, fr frame.Frame, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1229		pr := ps.peer
1230		switch f := fr.(type) {
1231		case frame.Data:
1232			// Decode already returned a fresh copy of the payload (it aliases nothing
1233			// else), so the resequencer may take ownership of it directly.
1234			rq := pr.resequencer.Load()
1235			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1236				// FEC on (T24): offer the data shard to the decoder BEFORE resequencing so a
1237				// later parity frame can reconstruct any group-mate lost in transit, then
1238				// deliver THIS received frame in its own right (the decoder never echoes a
1239				// directly-received data shard back). The shard's coded bytes are
1240				// OuterSeq || Payload — the same bytes the sender coded parity over.
1241				shard := fec.DataShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.FECIndex), Payload: fecShardPayload(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload)}
1242				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1243				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1244				// Residual-loss accounting (T29): this outer-seq was natively delivered, so mark
1245				// it present in the post-recovery loss estimator. A seq never marked here nor via
1246				// a reconstruction below is loss that FEC did not mask.
1247				if fr.connLoss != nil {
1248					fr.connLoss.Observe(f.OuterSeq)
1249				}
1250				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
1251			} else {
1252				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1253			}
1254		case frame.Parity:
1255			// PARITY feeds the FEC decoder (T24); a group that has now accumulated enough
1256			// shards reconstructs its missing data frames, each resequenced at its ORIGINAL
1257			// outer-seq (carried in the recovered shard's coded bytes) so recovery composes
1258			// with T18 exactly like a natively-received frame. With FEC off, PARITY is
1259			// dropped (the pre-T24 behaviour).
1260			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1261				shard := fec.ParityShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.ParityIndex), DataCount: int(f.DataCount), Payload: f.Payload}
1262				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1263				m.observeRecovered(fr, pr.resequencer.Load(), recovered, srcAP)
1264			}
1265		case frame.Probe:
1266			// Authenticated (the PROBE MAC verified in Decode): learn the return remote
1267			// from it, below the engine's virtual endpoint (no roaming churn).
1268			ps.setRemote(srcAP)
1269			if f.IsEcho {
1270				if ps.prober != nil {
1271					// A replay/forgery/wrong-path echo is rejected inside HandleEcho and
1272					// leaves liveness untouched; the error is a per-frame drop, not fatal.
1273					// ps.prober is the path's OWN immutable prober — never a lookup into a
1274					// dynamically-mutated slice — so runtime add/remove cannot race this.
1275					_ = ps.prober.HandleEcho(raw)
1276				}
1277				return
1278			}
1279			if pr.reflector != nil {
1280				if echo, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {
1281					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe, so this receive-goroutine reflection
1282					// races no in-flight Send on the same socket.
1283					_, _ = ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(echo, srcAP)
1284				}
1285			}
1286		default:
1287			// CONTROL (and any unhandled kind) is not delivered up this path.
1288		}
1289	}
1290	
1291	// observeRecovered feeds every FEC-reconstructed data frame into the resequencer at
1292	// its ORIGINAL outer-seq (recovered from the shard's coded bytes) via the NON-resyncing
1293	// ObserveRecovered path, so a frame rebuilt from parity resequences identically to a
1294	// natively-received frame — filling the exact gap the resequencer would otherwise time
1295	// out on — WITHOUT a late batch of recovered seqs being able to move the release point
1296	// or dump the live buffer (recovery must never cause loss). Only frames actually placed
1297	// AHEAD of the release point advance the honest delivered-recovery counter; a frame
1298	// rebuilt after the resequencer already skipped its gap is dropped as late and not
1299	// counted, so /metrics reflects delivered recovery, not mere reconstruction. A
1300	// malformed recovered shard (too short to hold the outer-seq prefix) is dropped: it
1301	// signals an encoder/decoder mismatch, not a deliverable frame.
1302	func (m *Multipath) observeRecovered(fr *fecReceiver, rq *reseq.Resequencer, recovered []fec.Recovered, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1303		for _, rec := range recovered {
1304			seq, inner, err := splitFECShardPayload(rec.Payload)
1305			if err != nil {
1306				continue
1307			}
1308			// Residual-loss accounting (T29): FEC reconstructed this outer-seq, so it is NOT
1309			// residual loss — mark it present in the post-recovery estimator even if the
1310			// resequencer later drops it as late (that is a latency outcome, not a masking
1311			// failure; the P4 residual bound measures loss FEC failed to mask).
1312			if fr.connLoss != nil {
1313				fr.connLoss.Observe(seq)
1314			}

MOTIVATION. Today a concentrator is single-peer-per-process. The config schema and the WireGuard engine already accept multiple [[wireguard.peers]] (config.validate only requires >=1; device.go uapiConfig ranges over all peers), but wanbond's multipath Bind is a single-tunnel bonding instance: internal/bind/multipath.go holds ONE virtual endpoint (m.virt, pinned to the first source it learns), ONE receive resequencer (m.resequencer, keyed purely by outer-seq with no peer separation), ONE send counter (m.outerSeq), ONE scheduler, ONE fecSend/fecRecv, ONE flat m.paths list, and ONE defaultRemote. So with two edges, their independent outer-seq spaces interleave into one resequencer release window (D32-class drops), the single virtual endpoint + per-path endpoint-learning clobber, and return traffic misroutes. Design rule A1 already SAYS 'one virtual endpoint per peer' — the code took the single-peer shortcut. The plural [[wireguard.peers]] schema is therefore misleading and should either work or be documented as unsupported.

CHOSEN DIRECTION (to confirm/refine in planning): approach (2) — AUTHENTICATED path->peer binding, NOT a forgeable outer-frame tunnel id. The crux is that the Bind demuxes frames BELOW the crypto layer (it resequences/FEC-recovers before the engine decrypts), so it cannot use the engine's Noise peer identity, and DATA frames carry no peer id (frame.go: OuterSeq/PathID/FEC only) and are unauthenticated by design (invariant 4) — an outer tunnel id would be spoofable across edges (cross-peer resequencer-injection DoS). The recommended enabler is a PER-PEER PSK (move psk from top-level into [[wireguard.peers]]), so the PSK-HMAC PROBE/CONTROL plane authenticates the SPECIFIC peer and can establish a path->peer binding; DATA frames on that path then route to that peer's resequencer. Then de-singleton the Bind: make virt / resequencer / outerSeq / scheduler / fecSend / fecRecv / paths / defaultRemote / probers / reflector per-peer (a map[peer]peerState), with the engine-facing ReceiveFunc demuxing each frame to the right peerState and Send(bufs, endpoint) routing via an endpoint->peerState map.

KNOWN REFACTOR SURFACE: internal/bind/multipath.go (the de-singleton + demux), internal/bind/bind.go (keep the conn-seam isolation), internal/reseq (per-peer instances), internal/sched + internal/fec + internal/telemetry (per-peer), internal/frame (only if a wire change proves necessary), internal/device/device.go (program the path->peer demux table from authenticated peer events; per-peer virtual endpoints to the engine), internal/config (per-peer psk + validation).

INVARIANTS TO PRESERVE (do not break): A1 one-virtual-endpoint-per-peer (now literally per-peer); own outer-seq space per sender (never the inner WG counter); resequence before inner anti-replay; DATA/PARITY unauthenticated by design (do not add a forgeable demux that weakens this); PROBE/CONTROL PSK-HMAC authenticated with monotonic anti-replay; conn coupling stays isolated to internal/bind/bind.go; amnezia all-or-nothing + single-engine-per-process; reedsolomon prefix-stability (TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant) on any FEC touch.

OPEN QUESTIONS FOR CLARIFICATION (planner should ask before designing):
- Scope: concentrator-side multi-peer only, or also edge talking to multiple DISTINCT concentrators simultaneously (different from the existing Q18 single-active-hub failover)? Recommend concentrator-only for this goal; edge-multi-hub is a separate feature.
- PSK model: per-peer PSK (recommended, enables authenticated demux) vs keep deployment-wide PSK + some other demux? What's the migration/back-compat story for existing single-peer configs (top-level psk)?
- Demux bootstrapping: how is a path attributed to a peer for the very FIRST frames, before any authenticated PROBE / before the WG handshake completes? (provisional/quarantine resequencer? gate DATA until an authenticated PROBE binds the path?)
- Roaming: when an edge's source rebinds (NAT), how does the path re-bind to the same peer without a window where frames misroute?
- Resource limits: max peers per concentrator; per-peer memory (a ~2048-frame resequencer ring + FEC state each); backpressure / eviction of idle peers; is there a configured cap?
- Security: can a malicious edge (that knows ITS psk) disrupt ANOTHER edge's tunnel? Threat-model the path->peer binding.
- Metrics: per-peer label on the /metrics series (wanbond_path_* etc.).

NON-GOALS: mesh/anycast; edge-side simultaneous multi-concentrator aggregation; changing the single-engine-per-process model; any DPI/obfuscation change.

TESTING DIRECTION: netns e2e with 2+ edges to one concentrator proving per-peer isolation (each edge's traffic resequences independently; one edge's loss/reorder/restart does not corrupt another's stream; return traffic routes to the correct edge); a per-peer resequencer unit test (two interleaved outer-seq streams stay separated); realhosts extension if feasible. Report-only real-link where absolute numbers apply, per the existing M10/Q12 discipline.","sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260713-210054-acde8de5f9cf22718.md",".cq/logs/20260713-223042-a2b09d2ae330ca1d8.md",".cq/logs/20260713-223042-a2c165322dcecdcd0.md",".cq/logs/20260713-223626-a79ddfeb2486cc79e.md",".cq/logs/20260713-224024-ac5e7e9c498fab1f8.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260713-210054-acde8de5f9cf22718.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260713-223042-a2b09d2ae330ca1d8.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260713-223042-a2c165322dcecdcd0.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260713-223626-a79ddfeb2486cc79e.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260713-224024-ac5e7e9c498fab1f8.jsonl"],"milestones":["M23","M24","M25","M26","M27"],"grounding":"Synthesized from 2 configured candidate planners (opus, fable; generate-N-then-judge per Q100/Q101; base = opus's 5-milestone/20-task candidate for its finer per-task testability, D32-per-peer isolation task, and the FEC prefix-stability invariant re-check the goal mandates; folded in from fable: the explicit pathState split into SHARED socket state vs per-(peer,path) codec/remote/prober/tx/rx — concentrator sockets are shared across peers — plus edge-role-with->1-peer rejection (Q21), uapiConfig golden byte-identity acceptance, (peer,path)-keyed metrics rate maps, and lazy per-peer state instantiation). Design keeps a SINGLE ReceiveFunc that stamps each delivered inner datagram with its peer's virtual endpoint (A1 literally per-peer) and demuxes Send via an endpoint->peerState map — no per-peer receive goroutines. Sharpest design point: the shared-socket vs per-(peer,path) path/remote model, isolated in M24/M25 where it is unit-testable before the e2e locks it in."},"createdAt":"2026-07-13T20:56:20.857Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-13T22:40:39.203Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"},"score":152.56163341299117,"matchedFields":["body"]}]}
